FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10, N=8267 early adolescents. Analyzed data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (Years 0-1 follow-up)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with multiple linear regression models examining associations between screen time measures (self-reported) at baseline and 1-year follow-up and caregiver-reported nutrition assessments at year 1

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time separated by modality: television, video games, videos (YouTube), texting, social media measured as continuous variables (hours/day). Paired with MIND diet nutrition score, a behavioral/health outcome. Qualifies because variables isolate phone/SM modalities and test association with individual-trait health outcome

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients reported for all modalities: total screen time β=-0.07 (95% CI -0.09, -0.05); videos β=-0.18 (95% CI -0.24, -0.12); texting β=-0.15 (95% CI -0.24, -0.05); social media β=-0.16 (95% CI -0.28, -0.05) in both prospective and cross-sectional analyses

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantitative results throughout: regression β coefficients with 95% CIs reported for all screen time modalities and nutrition score association

FT_EC7_reason: Design includes both prospective and cross-sectional analyses; screen time is tested as independent variable in main analyses; not a longitudinal SM-as-DV-only design
notes: All criteria met. Paper analyzes contemporary screen modalities (texts, social media) separately and finds associations with nutrition, demonstrating that smartphone/social media use variables are properly measured and paired with health outcome

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 follow-up, 2019-2021, N=9859, mostly 12-13 years old. Cross-sectional data from ABCD Study (5.0 release)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with multiple linear regression models estimating associations between transgender/gender-questioning identity and screen use (recreational screen time and problematic use measures)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Annals of Epidemiology 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time modalities measured separately: television/movies, single-player gaming, multi-player gaming, texting, social media, video chat, internet browsing. Problematic use scales (SMAQ, VGAQ, MPIQ) assess social media, video game, and mobile phone addiction. Paired with gender identity (transgender vs. gender-questioning vs. cisgender), an individual-trait demographic/identity variable. Qualifies because phone/SM variables tested as outcomes associated with an individual characteristic

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients reported: transgender vs. cisgender B=4.51 (95% CI 1.17-7.85) for total screen time; television B=0.82 (95% CI 0.16-1.49); social media B=0.83 (95% CI 0.06-1.61); SMAQ B=0.43 (95% CI 0.17-0.69); VGAQ B=0.46 (95% CI 0.19-0.74); MPIQ B=0.34 (95% CI 0.07-0.61)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results throughout: β coefficients with 95% CIs for all screen time modalities and problematic use scales

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (Year 3 snapshot), not longitudinal. FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design (baseline predicting later wave)
notes: All criteria met. Paper tests both recreational screen time and problematic use scales (SMAQ, VGAQ, MPIQ) separately, confirming phone/SM operationalizations paired with individual-trait variable

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Years 2-3, 2018-2021, N=9,398, ages 11-12 years at baseline. Prospective cohort data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with regression models (logistic, ordinal logistic, linear) examining prospective associations between bedtime screen use behaviors (Year 2) and sleep outcomes (Year 3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Adolescent Health 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use measured by modality: watching TV/movies, video games, music, talking/texting, social media, chat rooms, internet browsing, computer/laptop for studying, reading. Device measures: TV or Internet-connected device in bedroom, phone ringer status. All paired with sleep outcomes (sleep disturbance, sleep duration, disorders of initiating/maintaining sleep), an individual-trait health/behavioral outcome. Qualifies because phone/SM variables isolate device-specific or platform-specific use

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients and odds ratios reported: TV/electronic device in bedroom and sleep duration B=-0.21 (95% CI -0.28, -0.13); phone ringer on vs. off and sleep disturbance OR=1.25 (95% CI 1.00-1.56); talking/texting and sleep disturbance OR=1.13 (95% CI 1.05-1.23); social media and sleep duration B=-0.21 (95% CI -0.26, -0.16)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results throughout: β coefficients and ORs with 95% CIs for all bedtime screen activities and sleep outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal but screen/phone use is the independent variable (predictor) in all analyses, not the dependent variable. FT-EC7 requires SM as DV in EVERY analysis with no SM-as-IV anywhere. Here SM/phone consistently functions as IV
notes: All criteria met. Paper is prospective cohort testing bedtime-specific screen use (TV, social media, texting, etc.) as predictor of subsequent sleep outcomes
